subject: ap-world-history
title: Gunpowder Empires — Ottoman, Safavid, Mughal
stimulus:
question on the lined page designated for that response. Each response is expected to fit within the space provided.
In your responses, be sure to address all parts of the questions you answer. Use complete sentences; an outline or
bulleted list alone is not acceptable. You may plan your answers in this exam booklet, but no credit will be given for
notes written in this booklet.
“Late Ottoman society underwent significant modernization, a process that was both caused and accelerated
by the state reorganization program, Tanzimat, beginning in 1839. . . . Modern-educated Ottomans soon
began to emphasize in their writings the importance of European sciences. . . . Science was seen by many
officials and learned figures as the only tool to solve the problems of the empire. . . . This elite group of
Westernized Ottoman intellectuals . . . even adopted the belief in the supremacy of science in all aspects of
life and proposed to adopt a completely [Westernized] worldview.
However, this [Westernized Ottoman] elite was not the only group that focused on modern European thought.
Extreme views . . . sparked opposition, especially among religious circles, but they also kindled a general
curiosity among . . . a new generation of modern Ottoman religious scholars who emphasized an alternative
approach toward modernization. [These scholars] proposed to combine their traditional heritage with modern
ideas and methods . . . [and], while advocating the adoption of industrialization and political reforms, rejected
a cultural identification with the West.”
M.Sait Özervarli, historian, “Alternative Approaches to Modernization in the
Late Ottoman Period,” scholarly article, published in 2007

parts.prompt:
Identify ONE nineteenth-century development in the Ottoman Empire that led to the launch of the
reform program mentioned in the first paragraph.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE 19th-century development in the Ottoman Empire that led to the Tanzimat reform program. Typical AP-acceptable responses include: military defeats at the hands of European powers (e.g., the Greek War of Independence 1821–1832 or territorial losses to Russia); Ottoman loss of control over peripheral provinces (Egypt under Muhammad Ali, Balkans); European economic and technological superiority producing fiscal pressure; Sultan Mahmud II's earlier destruction of the Janissaries (1826) and opening toward Western-style military reform.

sample_responses.response: The Ottoman Empire's loss of Egypt to Muhammad Ali's effectively independent regime, combined with mounting military defeats to European powers in the early 19th century, demonstrated that the existing Ottoman administrative and military system could not compete — these concrete failures pressured Sultan Abdülmecid I to launch the Tanzimat reforms in 1839 as a top-down modernization program.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific 19th-century development (loss of Egyptian control plus military defeats) and causally ties it to the launch of Tanzimat. Historically accurate.

sample_responses.response: Sultan Mahmud II's destruction of the Janissary corps in 1826 removed the most entrenched institutional obstacle to military and administrative reform in the Ottoman Empire — clearing the path for his successor to launch Tanzimat in 1839 along Western-educated bureaucratic lines.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific earlier 19th-century development (Janissary abolition) that enabled the reform program the passage references. Historically accurate.
Restates the outcome described in the passage rather than identifying a specific development that led to the reform program.

required_elements: Historically defensible response addressing the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: Historically defensible response addressing the prompt.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.

parts.prompt:
Identify ONE argument made in the passage concerning the Ottoman elites and modernization in the
nineteenth century.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE argument the passage makes about Ottoman elites and modernization. Acceptable responses draw from the passage's two key claims: (1) a Westernized elite emerged that embraced European science and proposed a fully Westernized worldview; (2) a parallel group of modern Ottoman religious scholars proposed combining traditional heritage with modern ideas and methods while rejecting cultural Westernization.

sample_responses.response: One argument the passage makes is that a group of Westernized Ottoman intellectuals embraced European science so wholeheartedly that they proposed adopting a completely Westernized worldview, treating science as the only tool for solving the empire's problems.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific argument from the first paragraph of the passage. Closely tied to the text.

sample_responses.response: The passage argues that a second group of Ottoman religious scholars pursued an alternative modernization path — advocating industrialization and political reform while blending their traditional religious heritage with modern methods and rejecting a full cultural identification with the West.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the second paragraph's central claim about religiously-informed modernizers. Closely drawn from the passage.

sample_responses.response: The passage is about Ottoman modernization.
sample_responses.commentary: Restates the topic without identifying any specific argument the author makes.

required_elements: Historically defensible response addressing the prompt.

Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.

parts.prompt:
Explain how ONE OTHER non-Western state’s attempts to modernize in the nineteenth century led
to conflicts among members of the elite similar to those described in the passage.
2
“We consider Buddhists to be a direct threat. For over 1500 years, Buddhism has been spreading in our
kingdom, yet neither happiness nor profit has been gained. This is recorded in the historical books, which
Your Majesty has certainly studied thoroughly. Must you wait for your ministers to tell you?
We think of all the unorthodox teachings, Buddhism is the worst. The Buddhists live alone with their barbaric
customs, apart from the common productive population; yet they cause the people to be poor and to steal.
What is worse than their crimes? Beasts and birds that damage grain are certainly chased away because they
harm the people. Yet even though beasts and birds eat the people’s food, they are nevertheless useful to the
people. Buddhists, however, sit around and eat, and there has not yet been a visible profit.”
Yun Hoe, Korean Confucian scholar and adviser to the Korean Choson
monarchy, On the Harmfulness of Buddhism, 1424

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining how ONE other non-Western state's 19th-century modernization attempts produced elite conflicts similar to the Ottoman case. AP-acceptable examples include: Meiji Japan (traditionalist samurai versus Westernizing oligarchs, culminating in the Satsuma Rebellion 1877); Qing China (Self-Strengthening Movement conservatives versus reformers, especially around the Hundred Days' Reform 1898); late Romanov Russia (Slavophiles versus Westernizers); Muhammad Ali's Egypt (traditional ulama versus modernizing military-bureaucratic elite).

sample_responses.response: In Meiji Japan, the oligarchs' aggressive Westernization — abolishing samurai privileges, adopting European military organization, and industrializing — provoked sharp elite conflict with samurai traditionalists who saw their social rank and cultural identity erased; this tension erupted into the Satsuma Rebellion of 1877, paralleling the Ottoman split between Westernizers and religious-traditionalist modernizers.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains a specific parallel case (Meiji Japan) with a concrete event (Satsuma Rebellion) and explicitly ties the elite conflict pattern to the Ottoman case in the passage.

sample_responses.response: Qing China's Self-Strengthening Movement produced exactly the kind of elite division the passage describes in the Ottoman Empire: reformers like Li Hongzhang pushed for adopting Western military technology and industrial methods, while conservative Confucian scholar-officials resisted cultural imports — culminating in the failed 1898 Hundred Days' Reform, which the Empress Dowager Cixi crushed by siding with conservatives.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific comparable state (Qing China) and explains the elite modernizer-vs-traditionalist conflict with concrete examples and a specific event (Hundred Days' Reform).
Too vague; does not identify a specific state or explain the elite conflict in parallel to the Ottoman case.
